text:
The most elegant simple tool I can think of is the knot, with the wedge a very close second.

A knot is barely a tool at all in the usual sense, since it has no fixed form and is made entirely of the rope acting on itself. Yet with nothing but a length of cord and a particular way of folding it through itself, you can multiply your strength, hang a bridge, secure a sail, set a splint, or hold two things together with a bond that grips harder the more it is pulled and yet releases instantly when you tug the right end. There are no moving parts to break and no material that wears differently from the rope itself, since the whole of its function lives in geometry and friction. A good knot is information rather than substance, which is why the same trick passed reliably from hand to hand for thousands of years before anyone could write it down.

If you want something more obviously a tool, with a body you can hold, I keep returning to the wedge. The wedge is arguably the most elegant of all, because it is the purest case of trading distance for force. It is just an inclined plane that you carry with you, a triangle of hard material, and from that single idea come the axe, the chisel, the nail, the knife edge, the splitting maul, and even the teeth in your own mouth. There is nothing to adjust and nothing to understand beyond the angle, and yet it underlies most of how humans have ever cut, shaped, and fastened the physical world.

What unites my favorites is that the elegance comes from doing a great deal with almost no mechanism. The best simple tools are not clever so much as honest: they expose a single principle of physics and let you apply it directly with your own body. The knot gives you friction and topology, the wedge gives you the inclined plane, the lever gives you the moment arm. Each is close to irreducible, hard to improve without turning it into a different and more fragile thing, and that quality of being already at the bottom is, to me, exactly what elegance means.
